title: World Heritage Committee

infobox.value: 1992 | 7–14 December | [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") [Santa Fe](/wiki/Santa_Fe,_New_Mexico "Santa Fe, New Mexico")

is a committee of the [United Nations Educational, Scientific and Cultural Organization](/wiki/United_Nations_Educational,_Scientific_and_Cultural_Organization "United Nations Educational, Scientific and Cultural Organization") that selects the sites to be listed as UNESCO [World Heritage Sites](/wiki/World_Heritage_Site "World Heritage Site"), including the World Heritage List and the List of World Heritage in Danger, defines the use of the World Heritage Fund and allocates financial assistance upon requests from States Parties.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1) It comprises representatives from 21 [state parties](/wiki/Sovereign_state "Sovereign state")[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1) that are elected by the **General Assembly of States Parties** for a four-year term.[[3]](#cite_note-world-3) These parties vote on decisions and proposals related to the [World Heritage Convention](/wiki/World_Heritage_Convention "World Heritage Convention") and World Heritage List.
Originally scheduled for 2020. Postponed to an extended 2021 session due to the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic").[[8]](#cite_note-8)

Originally scheduled for 19–30 June 2022 in [Kazan](/wiki/Kazan "Kazan"), Russia. Postponed to an extended 2023 session due to the [Russian invasion of Ukraine](/wiki/2022_Russian_invasion_of_Ukraine "2022 Russian invasion of Ukraine").[[9]](#cite_note-10)[[10]](#cite_note-11)

outgoing_links.text: United Nations Educational, Scientific and Cultural Organization
outgoing_links.target_title: United Nations Educational, Scientific and Cultural Organization
International Union for Conservation of Nature
International Council on Monuments and Sites
International Centre for the Study of the Preservation and Restoration of Cultural Property

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Body selecting UNESCO World Heritage Sites

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ce/World_Heritage_Logo_global.svg/250px-World_Heritage_Logo_global.svg.png)](/wiki/File:World_Heritage_Logo_global.svg)

[Logo](/wiki/Logo "Logo") of the [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO") World Heritage Committee

The **World Heritage Committee** is a committee of the [United Nations Educational, Scientific and Cultural Organization](/wiki/United_Nations_Educational,_Scientific_and_Cultural_Organization "United Nations Educational, Scientific and Cultural Organization") that selects the sites to be listed as UNESCO [World Heritage Sites](/wiki/World_Heritage_Site "World Heritage Site"), including the World Heritage List and the List of World Heritage in Danger, defines the use of the World Heritage Fund and allocates financial assistance upon requests from States Parties.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1) It comprises representatives from 21 [state parties](/wiki/Sovereign_state "Sovereign state")[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1) that are elected by the **General Assembly of States Parties** for a four-year term.[[3]](#cite_note-world-3) These parties vote on decisions and proposals related to the [World Heritage Convention](/wiki/World_Heritage_Convention "World Heritage Convention") and World Heritage List.

According to the World Heritage Convention, a committee member's term of office is six years. However many States Parties choose to voluntarily limit their term to four years, in order to give other States Parties an opportunity to serve.[[3]](#cite_note-world-3) All members elected at the 15th [General Assembly](/wiki/General_assembly "General assembly") (2005) voluntarily chose to reduce their term of office from six to four years.[[3]](#cite_note-world-3)

Deliberations of the World Heritage Committee are aided by three advisory bodies, the [IUCN](/wiki/International_Union_for_Conservation_of_Nature "International Union for Conservation of Nature"), [ICOMOS](/wiki/International_Council_on_Monuments_and_Sites "International Council on Monuments and Sites") and [ICCROM](/wiki/International_Centre_for_the_Study_of_the_Preservation_and_Restoration_of_Cultural_Property "International Centre for the Study of the Preservation and Restoration of Cultural Property").[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-:1-5)

## Sessions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=World_Heritage_Committee&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Sessions")]

See also: [List of World Heritage Sites by year of inscription](/wiki/List_of_World_Heritage_Sites_by_year_of_inscription "List of World Heritage Sites by year of inscription")

The World Heritage Committee meets once a year for an ordinary session to discuss the management of existing [World Heritage Sites](/wiki/World_Heritage_Sites_by_country "World Heritage Sites by country"), and accept nominations by countries.[[3]](#cite_note-world-3) Extraordinary meetings can be convened at the request of two-thirds of the state members.[[6]](#cite_note-:2-6) Meetings are held within the territory of state members of the World Heritage Committee at their invitation. Rotation between regions and cultures is a consideration for selection and the location for the next session is chosen by the committee at the end of each session.[[6]](#cite_note-:2-6)

## Bureau

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=World_Heritage_Committee&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Bureau")]

At the end of each ordinary session, the committee elects a chairperson, five vice-chairpersons and a [Rapporteur](/wiki/Rapporteur "Rapporteur") from those members whose term will continue through the next session.[[6]](#cite_note-:2-6) These are known as the Bureau, and their representatives are responsible for coordinating the work of the World Heritage Committee, including fixing dates, hours and the order of business meetings.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)

## Voting

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=World_Heritage_Committee&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Voting")]

Each state member of the World Heritage Committee has one vote. Decisions require a simple majority with abstentions counted as not voting. Votes are delivered by a show of hands unless a secret ballot is requested by either the chairperson or two or more states members.[[6]](#cite_note-:2-6)

## Criticism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=World_Heritage_Committee&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Criticism")]

Increasing politicization of World Heritage Committee decisions to the detriment of conservation aims has been alleged, particularly with regard to new nominations for the World Heritage List, but also with the consideration of sites for the List of World Heritage in Danger.[[12]](#cite_note-14)[[13]](#cite_note-15) In 2010, states parties including Hungary, Switzerland and Zimbabwe submitted an official protest against such politicization.[[5]](#cite_note-:1-5)

An external audit requested by the World Heritage Committee for its *Global Strategy of the World Heritage List* concluded in 2011 that political considerations were indeed influencing decisions.[[5]](#cite_note-:1-5) It observed that the composition of committee representatives had shifted from experts to diplomats in spite of World Heritage Convention Article 9 and found that opinions from advisory bodies often diverged from World Heritage Committee decisions.[[5]](#cite_note-:1-5)

In 2016, Israel recalled its UNESCO ambassador after the World Heritage Committee adopted a resolution in a secret ballot that referred to one of [Jerusalem](/wiki/Jerusalem "Jerusalem")'s holiest sites, the [Temple Mount](/wiki/Temple_Mount "Temple Mount"), only as a "Muslim holy site of worship", not mentioning that Jews and Christians venerate the site.[[14]](#cite_note-16)[[15]](#cite_note-17)

The committee has also been criticized with alleged racism, colorism, and geographic bias for favoring the inscription of sites in Western and industrialized countries over sites belonging to so-called "third-world" countries. A large proportion of the world heritage sites are located in Europe, Eastern Asia, and North America, where populations notably have lighter skin.[[16]](#cite_note-18)[[17]](#cite_note-19)[[18]](#cite_note-20)[[19]](#cite_note-21)
